coredata.dc:title: Insights of nanomaterials integrated analytical approaches for detection of plant hormones in agricultural and environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Raval, Juhi Bhadresh
Mehta, Vaibhavkumar N.
Singhal, Rakesh Kumar
Basu, Hirakendu
Jha, Sanjay
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Hormones are an important class of biomolecules as they regulate the physiological responses in the living organisms. Recently, nanomaterials-based biosensors have been widely researched due to their outstanding merits like stabilty, selectivity, biocompatibility, facile synthetic approach, and cost-effectiveness. In this review, we cover in detail the recent advancements of nanomaterials (carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, metal-organic frameworks, metal nanoclusters and quantum dots) in electrochemcial, colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of plant hormones. This review also provides a brief outline on the classificaiton of phytohormones and the sample preparation appraoches for the extraction of phytohormones prior to their identification by various analytical techniques. This review will focus on the selected research papers on nanomaterials as electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the plant hormones detection from the last twelve years (i.e., 2011 −2023). Nanomaterials integrated analytical techniques (electrochemcial, colorimetric and fluorescence) offer to detect plat hormones with lower detection limits (fM to µM and ng/mL to pg/mL). Importantly, nanomaterials integrated analytical strategies have been successfully detected plant hormones with minimal volumes and sample preparations. The various advantages and limitations of techniques have been also overviewed. The challenges and future perspectives of nanomaterials-based electro- and optical sensors for the analysis of plant hormones are discussed in detail.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview on plant hormones and their functions in plants.

row.entry: Major role in elongation of stems and roots, suppresses the lateral buds growth with apical dominance, induces parthenocarpy, prevent premature falling of leaves, flowers, and fruits.

Inhibits seeds germination, activates senescence, encourages leaf and fruit abscission, and accelerates seed dormancy which is useful for storage purposes, plays a major role in stimulating stomatal closure to stop transpiration under water stress.

Encourage bolting, delays senescence, induces stem elongation and help in reversing dwarfism, it induces maleness in dioecious plants, induces the production of amylase and lipase enzymes in germinating seeds.
Plays a major in regulating abiotic and biotic stresses, senescence, tendril coiling, flower development, growth inhibition, and leaf abscission.
Major role in plant defence, control environmental stress conditions such as coldness, heat, dryness, stress of heavy elements, and ammonia tension and also helps to withstand salt stress.

Helps in developing lateral and adventitious shoot, used in tissue culture to initiate shoot development, reverse the auxin-induced apical dominance, increase the number of chloroplast, delays leaf senescence, and helps in movement of nutrients.

Enhances the fruit ripening, facilitates leaf epinastine, interrupt the bud and seed dormancy, helps in elongation of internodes and induces abscission and senescence of leaves and flowers, induces femininity in plants that are single, helps in development of apical hook in dicot seeds.
Helps in reproduction and etiolation, induces cell division, and expansion, plays a role in etiolation and reproduction.
Control plant metabolite concentrations, affect the plant architecture, helps in developing symbiotic relationship with the parasitic fungi, stimulates branching in plants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hormones are an important class of biomolecules as they regulate the physiological responses in the living organisms. Recently, nanomaterials-based biosensors have been widely researched due to their outstanding merits like stabilty, selectivity, biocompatibility, facile synthetic approach, and cost-effectiveness. In this review, we cover in detail the recent advancements of nanomaterials (carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, metal-organic frameworks, metal nanoclusters and quantum dots) in electrochemcial, colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of plant hormones. This review also provides a brief outline on the classificaiton of phytohormones and the sample preparation appraoches for the extraction of phytohormones prior to their identification by various analytical techniques. This review will focus on the selected research papers on nanomaterials as electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the plant hormones detection from the last twelve years (i.e., 2011 −2023). Nanomaterials integrated analytical techniques (electrochemcial, colorimetric and fluorescence) offer to detect plat hormones with lower detection limits (fM to µM and ng/mL to pg/mL). Importantly, nanomaterials integrated analytical strategies have been successfully detected plant hormones with minimal volumes and sample preparations. The various advantages and limitations of techniques have been also overviewed. The challenges and future perspectives of nanomaterials-based electro- and optical sensors for the analysis of plant hormones are discussed in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: A biosensor is a substantial advancement in scientific analysis that can be described as, a device that depends on specific biological and chemical reactions involving, immune systems, enzymes, organelles, tissues, or whole cells, at the time of detection of chemical compounds via optical, thermal, or electrical signals [1]. Biosensors promise to make biologically related substances detectable with high precision, cost effectiveness and in timely manner via biorecognition and signal transduction. Numerous scientists have tried to improve the performance of the sensor devices for the quick detection of antigens, antibodies, hormones, enzymes, DNA, and proteins which are present in complex biological samples. Recent advances in bioanalytical techniques have resulted in the integration of traditional molecular biology approaches with simple analytical techniques to create a convenient and portable system for on-site real time detection of interested analytes [2]. The impending use of biomolecules as disease biomarkers, biosensing entities have gained lots of consideration in the aftermath of those breakthroughs for shifting the medical paradigm from treatment to prevention and diagnosis. Biosensors based detection via analytical techniques have been widely employed for analysis of drugs, biomolecules, and complex metabolites. Various biosensor modes based on physico-chemical signal transducers including electrochemical, colorimetry, field-effect transistors (FET), fluorescence, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) have been developed for the detection of various target molecules from inorganic species to molecules (organic and biomolecules). These methods offers several advantages such as low detection limits, rapidity, simplicity and requirement of minimal volume of samples without major sample preparation or purifications. Recently, the nanomaterial-based biosensing approaches have been widely investigated in assaying of trace level analytes due to their outstanding properties, exploring them as the prime choice for employing in the development of miniaturized analytical devices with remarkable analytical features [3].

Various nanomaterials like nanosheets, nanotubes, nanoparticles (NPs), quantum dots (QDs), nanorods, carbon nanomaterials (carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs)), metal-organic framework (MOFs), and MXens are already explored in the detection of various biomolecules. Nowadays, the research paradigm has shifted to integrate nanostructure materials with simple analytical techniques (electrochemical, fluorescence and colorimetry) for recognition of hormones at the trace level [4]. The detection of hormones plays an important role in assessing many physiological processes in living organisms. These hormones helps in regulating and controlling various aspects in plants like bud dormancy, seed dormancy, increase in the cell division activity, growth of meristems etc. The change in the concentartion of the hormones may lead to various diseases which ultimately impact the crop yield. As the concentration of hormones is very less, it is very difficult to detect the hormones by using the conventional methods. Hence, it is of utmost important to develop the techniques which have excellent detection limit with selectivity at the trace level. In this connection, the detection of phyto-hormones have received the considerable interest in recent times. The increase in the number of publications in last few years indicates the tremendous efforts taken up by the researchers in developing analytical methods for the detection of phytohormones. Moreover, the rapid growth of research in plant hormones functions and analysis is evidenced by the progressive increase in the number of research papers published on plant hormones over the past 23 years ( Fig. 1). In view of this, this review will be restricted toward the research works that have been published especially on nanomaterials as electrochemical and optical sensors for the detection of plant hormones in the period of 2011 – 2023.

The main objective of this review is to provide some useful insights and directions in the field of nanomaterial-based biosensors for the plant hormone detection. This review emphasize on the various nanomaterials (metal NPs, QDs, CDs, GQDs, metal oxide NPs, metal NCs, MOFs and MXens) based analytical approaches (electrochemical, colorimetry and fluorescence) for the detection of plant hormones. It begins with highlighting the various plants hormones, followed by the detailed classification of plant hormones. The preceeding section gives an outline of the different techniques available for the detection of hormones such as immunoassays, mass spectrometry, chromatography, fluorescence, colorimetric and electrochemical techniques. Another section provides a detailed overview of major analytical techniques like electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescence analytical techniques for the detection of hormones. The conclusion and the future perspectives are also highlighted. This review covers the significant potential of nanomaterials-based biosensors for quantitative and qualitative plant hormones detection. The major objectives of this review as follows:
•
To give a brief introduction about plant hormones and sample preparation approaches for the extraction of plant hormones
•
To provide the insights of traditional analytical techniques for the detection of hormones and their prons and cons.
•
An in-depth discussion about nanomaterial based analytical techniques for sensing of plant hormones, focussing mainly on electroanalytical, colorimetic and fluorescence methods with the integration of various nanomaterials.
•
To highlight analtyical figures of functional nanostructures in the development of electrochemical and optical sensors for phytohormones assays.
•
To discuss significant challenges and future perspectives of nanostructure materials in the development of portable analytical devices for plant hormone detection.

Hormones are known as chemical messengers responsible for control and coordination in living organisms [4]. In plant, they are released from various plant cells and tissues according to the environmental signals, where they act on target tissues or organ mostly at a target site from their place of secretion. It carry essential messages that have far reaching effects. They have a vital role in synchronizing the behavior and physiology of a living entity by integrating, controlling and regulating its major function [5]. Basically, hormones resembles with certain chemical molecules such as neurotransmitters and cytokines [6]. Since the quantity of hormones is related to a variety of life activities, revealing an urgent need to establish a rapid, simple and sensitive analytical strategy for monitoring of hormones levels in plants and biofluids. In real time, hormones can now be detected using nanoparticles-based biosensors without the need for extensive sample preparation or treatment [7].

Based on the origin, hormones can be divided into two classes as phytohormones and animal hormones. The plant and animal hormones differ based on their structure. Phytohormones (plant hormones) are made up of simple organic molecules. Plant hormones are synthesized at the designated place and are then diffused to the target cell in the response to environmental stimuli by cell to cell interactions. This review breifly focusses on the importance of plant hormones and its trace level detection using nanomaterials as an efficient probes.

Plant hormones termed as phytohormones are signaling molecules, synthesized within plants, in very minute quantities. Plants are continually subjected to biotic and abiotic stressors, such as pathogenic bacteria and drought [8]. Plants have evolved complex and effective defense systems, such as distinctive immune responses and systemic acquired resistance, to protect themselves [9]. Small signalling molecules, such as phytohormones, are required to activate such defence pathways. Plant growth regulators are required for a variety of physiological functions in plants, including growth, development, reproduction, and stress response [10]. Hormone modulation could boost-up plant yields by managing crop size and shape, improving crop stress tolerance, and increasing grain output [11]. Preliminary research into phytohormones have identified the important phytohormones including ethylene, abscisic acid (ABA), cytokinins, auxins, and gibberellins as plant growth regulators and inhibitors. Furthermore, brassinosteroids, jasmonates, salicylic acid, and strigolactones are also considered as major phytohormones in plant science. Generally, the concenntration of phyohormones in the plant tissues are at very low concentraions, for example, indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) (10–100 ng/g), gibberellins (GAs) (1 −100 ng/g), cytokinins (1–100 ng/g), and ABA (10–50 ng/g). The classification of the plant hormones is depicted in Fig. 2. Table 1 depicts the structure, molecular weight and major functions of plant hormones.

The identification, monitoring and detection of phytohormones is necessary as they are involved in various growth and developmental process of plants. The analytical techniques have faciliated the efficent and sensitive detection of hormones, with quick sample preparation and purification of the hormones before the detection. The steps of pre-sample preparation/treatment is the major limitation for the analytical based detection for on-site, real time detection of the hormones [12]. Therefore, it is of ut-most important to choose a suitable method for the extraction and purification of hormones. The sample preparation method must be facile, fast, cost effective, and green i.e. with minimum solvent. The detection technique primarily depends on the fast recognition, confirmation, and quantification of analytes, particularly those present at trace or ultra-trace levels in complex matrices which could be compromised by the sample pre-treatment [13]. Additionally, as analytical chemistry develops, sample preparation increasingly plays a significant role in analysis and can consume up to 80% of the time in a full separation-based analytical process. Thus, sample preparation is an essential step in the characterization of hormones in plants and biological samples. Sample preparation for the measurement of hormones often entails many processes, including sampling, freeze drying, homogenization, thorough extraction from the matrix, and elimination of interference molecule by a number of cleanup techniques before instrumental analysis [14]. The distribution and concentrations of the hormones in plant is dependent on various environmental factors, which plays a key role in the hormonal detection. The solvent extraction is commonly used for the isolation of hormones.

For the extraction process, the specimen material is kept at lower temperature to avoid enzymatic degradation or oxidation of various chemical compounds [15]. Generally, the extraction of hormones from the sample involves the use of different solvent systems such as ethanol, ethanol/acetone, ethanol/water, ethanol/acetone/water, propanol, propanol/acetone, propanol/water, propanol/acetone/water, acetone:water mixture in different ratios. Moreover, polar solvents have been widely employed for the hormone extraction (methanol is the prime choice). In this connection, the Bieleski solvent (MeOH:CHCl3:H-COOH:H2O(12:5:1:2, v/v/v/v)) and modified Bieleski solvent ((MeOH:HCOOH: H2O (15:1:4, v/v/v)) have been identified as the major choice for the efficient extraction of phytohormones [16]. The physio-chemical properties, nature of the specimen, and nature of the hormone are some of the parameters which affects the choice of solvent. After the extraction process, numbers of purification steps are involved to remove the organic and inorganic contaminants which have been extracted during solvent extraction process. As hormones are in trace amounts, it is necessary to purify the extract before analytical determination of the hormones. The important purification techniques include liquid extraction, liquid-phase microextraction (LPME), solid-phase microextraction (SPME) and molecularly imprinted microextraction. The liquid extraction method is based on the polarity of the solvents which can be used to purify the polar hormones from non-polar compounds [17]. This technique suffers from the degradation of desired hormone due to the attack of the organic solvents at pH different from the physiological pH of plants [18]. This method is also time consuming and require large amount of solvent to get a purified compound. To overcame the drawbacks of liquid extraction, LPME method is commonly used as this method uses less amount of solvent and provides quick purification. This method was used to purify plant hormones from coconut juice [19].

Solid- phase extraction method is widely used as compared to liquid phase extraction due to its good recovery rate and easy selection of the sorbent material which consumes less solvent with low sample processsing time to get purified hormones [20]. It is also widely used to remove the interferring molecules from the sample. The development of new sorbent material is the new research hotspot which can help in more efficient removal of contaminants to extract the pure product. A similar method derived from solid-phase extraction is magnetic solid-phase extraction, which employs the use of magnetic particles [21]. This method is highly-reliable and time-saving. To attain the miniturization, a simple, reliable, facile, sensitive, selective and solvent-free SPME method has been evolved for the in-vitro isolation of plant hormones [22]. The in-vivo application of SPME is still the emerging area of research in plant biology [23]. Chemical labelling, and molecular imprinted extraction are similar methods employing a labelled molecule or a substance identical to the desired compound used for the purpose of purification [24]. Vapor-phase method, super critical fluid extraction and chromatographic methods are also widely used for the purification of the hormones [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical techniques for the identification of hormones
ce:section.ce:para: The progress in developing a highly selective and sensitive detection technology is crucial for obtaining information on endogenous hormone concentrations as the hormones are present at very trace levels in biological samples. For understanding the functions of hormones in signalling transduction networks and their molecular mechanisms, the analysis of hormones is very important. Despite the use of multistep purifications on crude extracts, the samples to be examined still contain a considerable number of interfering chemicals which allows false detection. A variety of unique approaches have been developed to determine hormones at trace levels from live samples. During the last decade, mass spectrometry, immunoassays, electroanalysis, capillary electrophoresis (CE), in particular, chromatography procedures like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), gas chromatography (GC), and bioassays, were mostly used techniques in hormone analysis. Moreover, analytical techniques have gained the tremendous interest among the researchers, particularly electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescence, owning to their simplicity, and sensitivity with specificity towards the interested analyte. A wide variety of traditional and modern analytical tools are available for the detection of the hormones. In this connection, GC-based approaches are presently the most widely utilised in phytohormone analysis due to the greater resolving capacity of capillary GC columns. HPLC is only utilised with highly selective samples or detectors that have been thoroughly purified [26]. A literature reports have shown the exceptional linearities within the ranges of 0.5–10 ng mL-1 – 5 – 500 ng mL-1 for BAP, zeatin, GA, kinetin, IAA and ABA, respectivley for the detection of several phytohormones using LC-MS [12].

Furthermore, antibody based immunoassays are highly sensitive and has been widely employed for detection of hormones. Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and radioimmunoassay (RIA) are two immunoassay techniques used to determine hormones. Immunoassay is the most extensively utilised rapid-detection technology due to its flexibility to many types of samples and ease of usage. The modern analytical techniques likes electroanalytical, surface-enhanced Raman Spectroscopy, colorimetric, and flurorescence based methods surpasses the disadvantages of the traditional methods making it a ideal choice for the hormones detection. Therefore, the reviewed analytical techniques have been widely used to detect the chemical messengers in plants due to its high senstivity and selectivity even at trace level concentrations of analytes.

In arrears to the extraordinary traits of nanomaterials, such as conductivity, optoelectronic properties, catalytic activity and biocompatibility, nanoparticle-based sensors have recently received a lot of interest for the sensitive and selective detection of analyte. Nanomaterials can be used to solve problems with conventional organic dye-based colorimetric systems. By causing color changes that are apparent to the naked eye, noble metal NPs, which have distinctive physicochemical and optical features and higher extinction coefficients than dyes, assist in the identification of the target analytes. The use of nanomaterials also improves the sensitivity of various simple spectrometric techniques (UV–visible and fluorescence). Recently, the nanomaterial-based sensors have been intensively investigated due to their detection ability at ultralow concentrations of target analytes with the naked eye [27]. Furthermore, their distinct SPR capabilities make them as excellent visual readers for the recognition of numerous analytes from molecules to cells [2,28]. The application of nanomaterials is influenced by a number of aspects, including their physical qualities and the fact that they have a higher surface area to volume ratio, which favors for the functionalization of nanostrucutres with new molecular architechtures [29]. Nanodots, nanofibers, nanodiamonds, nanohorns, nanotubes, CDs, QDs, NCs, and nanosheets are the most common kinds of nanomaterials with different dimensions and also found to have a great application in detection of hormones. Two-dimensional nanomaterials like graphene sheets and its derivatives have been also employed for electrochemical and fluorescence based sensing. The presence of oxygen carrying functional group enhances the chemical functionalization of graphene sheets making it a ideal choice for biosensors [30]. The graphene and its derivatives have also been used for fabricating wearable sensors for the detection of chemical and gas sensors [31]. A sensor for the detection of a plant growth regulator, alpha- napthalene acetic acid (NAA) was fabricated using self-assembled phosphorene/Ti3C2-MXene nanohybrid [32]. NAA hormone was electrochemically detected having a broad linear range of 0.02–40 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.6 nM. Salicylic acid, a phytohormone was detected using an antibody based detection method. The aptamer- based nanosensor was synthesized using a nanopore thin film with the LOD of 0.1 μM for salicylic acid with less than 30 min detection time [33]. This conjugate was cost effective, fast, reliable and can also be used for the detection of various other hormones or biomolecules.

An electrochemical biosensor is defined as a unified system that uses interactions between biochemical receptors and electrochemical transducer elements to provide accurate information about qualitative and quantitative profiles of a specified target [34]. The detection technique for electroanalytical based sensor is cost effective and reliable than traditional methods [35]. The advent of electrochemical sensing to function non-invasively and non-destructively, makes this detection technique a widely exploited analytical tool for living cells [36]. This method also have a add on advantage as it minimizes the use of harmful chemical agents and toxic dyes, mutagenic radio-labeled probes and fluroscent probes [37]. The progress of biosensors facilitates the detection of various biomolecules and other physiologically important biomaterials including hormones [38]. The electrochemical biosensing of hormones has prime application in maintaining the plant health and also have major effect on its production yield [39]. The amount of hormones that control and regulate the plant metabolism is very small, this is the most significant reason for the development of extremely sensitive techniques for its detection [40]. The electrochemical method has classically been employed for phytohormones sensing because it can conquer the limits of other ingrained methods like ELISA, immunochromatographic assay, liquid chromatography in terms of time performance, selectivity and sensitivity [41]. This section describes numerous electrochemical sensors developed recently for specific detection of plant hormones.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalytical techniques for the detection of plant hormones
ce:section.ce:para: ABA is an important phytohormone, that plays a vital role in inhibiting the plant growth [42]. It have a dominant effect in seed germination, stomatal closure, and adaptation to environmental stress [43]. ABA is also responsible for leaf shedding, bud dormancy, and inhibition of cell growth. ABA is vital for the normal functioning of plants, and thus the study of minute concentrations of ABA in plants has become even more essential [44]. Especially, monitoring of ABA levels in plants is essential to faciliate the growth process in plants and additionally aids in regulating their growth further. An electrochemical sensor that is non-destructive with good analytical figure has been intensively researched as a promising sensing platform that leverages the ABA receptor. A recent study uses vertical graphene (VG) film as a electrocatalyst and Au@SnO2 NPs as the receptor for ABA [45]. The Au@SnO2-VG microelectrodes sensor developed was highly specific, have long-term stability and sensitive showing a good linear concentration range of 0.012– 495.2 μM at a physiological pH range of 4–7. The LOD of ABA ranged between 0.002 μM and 0.005 μM. The real sample analysis was done using cucumber juice showing a good recovery percentage. A similar work based on the detection of ABA works on the principle of enzyme-linked competitive immunoreaction [46]. The reaction takes place between enzyme-labeled and free ABA to bind on antibodies conjugated with AuNPs on carbon electrode. The electrochemical signals are produced when horseradish peroxidase conjugated with anti-ABA antibody, in the presence of hydroxyquinone is reduced to hydrogen peroxide. The synthesized amperometric immunosensor shows high sensitivity and selectivity due to the presence of specific enzymes. Another phytohormone, 6- benzylaminopurine (6-BAP), is a well known naturally occurring cytokinin. It plays a vital role in regulation of many developmental stages and growth phase of plants. 6-BAP was detected using multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [47]. Stripping voltammetric methods was employed for detection of trace quantities of 6-BAP. This method exhibited different LODs (pH 10: 0.235 µM, pH 7.0:.165 µM, and pH 3.0: 0.326 µM) at different pH, lower LOD was noticed at pH 7.0. The authors reported that the real sample analysis using farmland soil, small vegetables, lettuce, tap water was more feasible when it was pretreated using acid solution. Another well known cytokinin, zeatin was detected using photo electrochemical detection. The sensor was fabricated using a photoactive material, graphene like carbon nitride and GQDs, which were attached to a AuNPs substrate [48]. In the presence of zeatin, DNA aptamers can form conjugates with zeatin, causing aptamer to discharge from the surface of electrode and a decrease in streptavidin capture, resulting in an increase in photoelectrochemical (PEC) response. The Fig. 3 illustrated the working principle of the synthesized biosensor for sensing of cytokinin zeatin. The biosensor had a broad linear range of 0.1–100 nM and a good detection limit of 0.031 nM under ideal conditions. The sensor was highly specific and can also identify the analogues of zeatin.

IAA is a key plant regulatory hormone which is present in very minute amount in the plant parts. IAA has major functions in cell elongation and differentiation. The IAA detection requires highly sensitive detection techniques due to trace amounts of hormone present in plants. An electrochemical based immunoassay which is based on specific antigen- antibody recognition, in which AuNPs decorated with horseradish peroxidase labeled IgG was used as signal amplication probe and rat monoclonal antibody was used as a capture probe for IAA [49]. This immunosensor was applied for the IAA detection in mung bean sprouts, the practical evidence reported that the sensor was highly efficient and showed the recovery rate of 93.5–106.75%. A analogous strategy employing magnetic NPs synthesized by 4-aminophenylboronic acid decorated with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated goat anti-rat immunoglobulin G (HRP-IgG-Fe3O4) and IAA-modified AuNPs (anti-IAA-AuNPs) was conjugated against rat monoclonal antibody [50]. The derived biosensor have excellent reproducibility, accuracy, selectivity, and excellent stability. For the real-time analysis of IAA, another photoelectrochemical biosensor, based on 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) stabilised by cadmium sulphide (CdS-NPs)/reduced graphene oxide (3-MPA-CdS-NPs@RGO) hybrid nanostructure was developed using solvothermal method [51]. The presence of 3-MPA aids in a uniform distribution of CdS NPs on the RGO sheets, which enhances the photo signals, making the sensor more sensitive and accurate. The synthesized sensor depicts a good lower LOD of 0.05 ng/mL and a broad range varying from 0.1 to 1000 ng/mL. A novel microsensor fabricated using highly ordered nanopores, PtNPs, popcorn-like Au nanostructures, and RGO nanocomposite films was employed for the detection of IAA [52]. This nanostructure mediated electrochemical sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity and exceptional sensitivity with limit of quantification of 143 pg mL− 1. The real sample analysis was done using LC-MS analysis by taking stem samples of soybean seedlings. A novel approach using toluidine blue (TB) functionalized MOFs based electrochemical immunosensor was employed for the detection of IAA [53]. The molecules of TB gets encapsulated into the MOFs. The elctrochemical signal is based on the amount of TB which gets encapsulated in the MOFs. This is a novel strategy which uses MOFs material for the electrochemical based sensing for biomolecules. The detection in real samples confirms the good stability and sensitivity of the proposed biosensor. Another phytohormone 2–4 Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) was detected using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fe3O4-polyaniline (Fe3O4-PANI) nanocomposite was integrated on glassy carbon electrode [54]. Cyclic voltammetric curves of bare electrodes in the presence of 0.9 µM 2,4-D shows no characteristic peak while the modified electrodes with Fe3O4-PANI showed a redox peaks confirming the electroactive behaviour and quasi-reversible electrode reaction. Nyquist plots were used to measure electron charge transfer resistance (Rct) of bare electrode and modified electrode. The lower Rct value for Fe3O4-PANI-GCE is a result of excellent conductivity of Fe3O4 and PANI in the nanocomposite. The electrochemical determination of 2,4-D shows a good linearity in the range of 1.35–2.7 μM. With the detection limit of 0.21 μM and sensitivity of 4.62 × 10−7 μA μM−1 cm−2, the fabricated sensor is cost effective, reliable and highly specific.

Methyl jasmonate is a plant growth regulator which helps in growth and development. MeJa promotes the production of phenolic compounds and anthocyanins in fruits, which aid in development of the lustre, color and the antioxidant capacity of fruits. An electrochemical based biosensor fabricated using ferrocene (Fc), COOH-MWCNTs and carboxylated graphene (COOH-GR) composite was synthesized on screen printed electrode (SPE) [55]. Fig. 4a illustrates the diagrammatic representation of fabrication of the electrochemical sensor. Fig. 4b shows the differential pulse voltammetric curves of different materials fabricated on SPE to compare the oxidation potential of various nanostructure materials. Fig. 4c displays the DPV curves after the addition of MeJA, Anti-MeJA and bovine serum albumin (BSA). The material Fc-GR-MWNT/SPE showed highest oxidation signal, hence was used in the sensor. The decrease in the oxidation peak current marks the detection of MeJa. The synthesized immunosensor has a linear range of 100 fM-100 μM, offering a lower LOD of 31.26 fM. The probe-free immunosensor was cheap, reliable and has a significant potential in precision agriculture.

A similar approach using nanosheets made from pyrite FeS2 was employed for the detection of methyl jasmonate [56]. This disposable sensor possess outstanding sensitivity in a linear concentration range of 1–2.5 mM with a exceptional detection limit of 0.68 mM. Such disposable electrochemical immunosensors opens up a new era for many analytical applications. There has been many literature available for simultaneous detection of plant growth regulators. A recent report based on AuNPs doped, graphene hydrogel modified electrode, working on the principal of electrochemical based biosensor was constructed for the detection of IAA and salicylic acid [57]. Under the given physiological conditions the biosensor shows a effective lower limits of detetction of 0.21 and of 0.22 μM for IAA and salicylic acid with a optimum linear ranges of 0.8–4 and of 0.8–8.4 μM for IAA and salicylic acid. Another similar approach using carbon tape modified electrode [58] and MWCNTs [59,60] was also reported. Their experimental evidences shows a good low limit of detetction range using electrochemical based biosensors. Recently, AuNPs@RGO nanocomposites were fabricated on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the development of wireless electrochemical biosensor for the detection of plant regulator IAA in plants [61]. In this work, authors used ultrasonication and hydrothermal methods to prepare AuNPs@RGO nanostructures and then coated to SPCE. A wireless electrochemical biosensor was developed for sensing of IAA by integrating AuNPs@RGO-SPCE with smartphone, exhibiting a dynamic linear ranges (0.25–120.0 μM and 135.0–500.0 μM) with a LOD of 0.15 μM. The developed wireless sensor was successfully demonstrated for the quantification of IAA in different parts (stems, buds and roots) of mung bean sprouts, which showed good aggreement with HPLC method. A sensitive salicylic acid electrochemical sensor was fabricated by modifying gold electrode with CuNPs [62]. The detection of salicylic acid was based on its electrocatalytic oxidative reaction in the presence of CuNPs modified gold electrode. The developed method was effectively applied to assay salicylic acid in Sclerotinia sclerotiorum infected oilseed rape. Another analytical approach demonstrated the use of carbon nano-onions in the development of electrochemical sensor for sensing of 2,4-D and 2,4,5-Trichloroacetic acid [63]. The selectivity and sensitivity of electrochemical sensor are greatly improved by modifying electrode with carbon nano-onion structures, favoring to assay both molecules in river water and soil samples. Table 2 shows various reported literature based on the detection of different plant hormones using electroanalytical techniques.

Colorimetric approaches are popular because of their ease of use and inexpensive cost. These sensors can usually be detected simply by looking at the color changes with the naked eye, thus no complicated or expensive extra instruments are required [64–66]. They can also be easily integrated with smartphone for on-site identificaiton of trace level molecular species [2]. Quick and low-cost colorimetric devices have proven to be effective in assaying of wide variety analytes in agriculture, environmental and biomedical research areas. The development of visual readout assays has accelerated in recent years, demonstrating the scientific community's keen interest in this topic. The difficulty of inventing new colorimetric sensors that allow apparent naked-eye detection of the analyte via significant color changes. Several metal and metal oxide nanostructures have been successfully used as probes for visual readout assays of various molecules, which offers impressive sensitivity and remarkable selectivity, just like as advanced analytical techniques [2,27,64]. Dye-based and nanoparticle-based sensors have been researched extensively. Dye-based colorimetric devices have long piqued interest due to their low cost, excellent stability, and versatility in monitoring trace level analytes in food, agriculture and biological samples. The color changes caused or mediated by an analyte following its response with the dye are the functional principle of these colorimetric sensors [65]. Unfortunately, due to the dyes relatively low extinction coefficients and the difficulty in recognising minor color alterations with the naked eye, these approaches are not extremely sensitive. Furthermore, determining specific components of a mixture without using specific ligand systems is frequently difficult [2]. The issues associated with standard organic dye-based colorimetric devices can be overcome using nanomaterials. The usage of nanomaterial-based colorimetric sensors that can detect ultralow concentrations of target analytes with the naked eye has been extensively researched in recent years [66]. Table 3 provides the information on the use of nanomaterials as probes for the colorimetric assay of plant hormones using various nanomaterials as probes.

GAs are biological molecules composed of tetracyclic diterpenoid acids that controls various physiological and developmental progressions in plants, like stem and leaf growth, seedling development, floral initiation, seed germination, fruit and flower growth [67]. A novel colorimetric detection method of GAs, using NPs was reported in various literatures. In a recent literature, the AuNPs were synthesized by reduction reaction with citrate ions [68]. The prominent surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band at 520 nm, confirms the brick red color of the freshly produced AuNPs. The SPR peak of AuNPs was bathochromically shifted to higher wavelengths of 700 nm after interaction with GAs. After its interaction with the phytohormone, the color of the AuNPs showed a alteration, the AuNPs color changed from brick red to purple. The AuNPs mediated detection of GAs shows a limit of detection of 0.2 ppm, with a consistent range of 0.1–1.0 ppm. The detection in real sample of rhizosphere of the tomato plant also shows a dramatic color change due to the presence of GA.

Another similar approach was used for the detection of a synthetic auxin IAA using AuNPs. Here the authors had labeled the AuNPs with IAA antibody [69]. The conjugate shows a good eye ball limit of detection of 0.01 ng/mL, with concentrations ranging from 0.02 to 30 ng/mL. The precise capability of visual detection using the naked eye, AuNP - based colorimetric detection techniques have gain immense expanding interest in recent years. Another approach for the detection of plant growth inhibitor ABA, employs AuNPs, which are capped with a pentapeptide Cys-Ala-Leu-Asn-Asn (CALNN) [70]. Here the colorimetric assay works on the principle of aggregation of NPs in the attendance of ABA specific antibody. The development of this aggregation caused the color change which can be seen through naked eyes. With the addition of ABA-antibody the color drastically change from purple to red. Fig. 5a illustrates the principle for the detection of ABA. Fig. 5b explains the UV–visible spectra of various concentrations of ABA-GE, ABA-AuNPs and with ABA antibody, with the inset image showing the visible color change. In a given optimal conditions the concentration was in a linear range from 5 nM to 10 μM, with a LOD of 2.2 nM. For identification of the cytokinin, zeatin hormone, a similar work was done using Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) capped with aptamers [71]. Single-stranded oligonucleotides (ssRNA or ssDNA) are termed as aptamers. Due to the aptamers unique adsorptive characteristics for AuNPs, conformational changes in the DNA or binding of the target allow the AuNPs to assemble and give a colorimetric signal. To protect AuNPs against salts, a trans-zeatin aptamer was utilised. As the amount of trans-zeatin increases, the color of AuNPs changes from blue to purple to red, this characteristic color change is used to detect the concentration of trans-zeatin. The ratio of absorbance was found to be (A522/A650), with a detection limit of 0.037 μM in a linear concentration of 0.05–0.75 μM. The synthesized colorimetric detector is reliable, cost effective and can be used without the employment of any expensive instruments.

For the detection of another major phytohormone 6-BAP, thioglycolic acid capped AgNPs (TGA-AgNPs) was synthesized [72]. The Fig. 6a shows a general scheme for the colorimetric based detection of 6-BAP, Fig. 6b depicts UV–visible spectra of citrate capped AgNPs, and AgNPs conjugated with TGA and Fig. 6c shows UV–vis spectra regarding the quenching observed in the presence of 6-BAP, the inset image shows the visible color change in the presence of phytohormone 6-BAP. This colorimetric sensor was highly efficent and shows high degree of sensitivity for the detection of 6-BAP. Furthermore, the colorimetric sensor was effectively used to detect 6-BAP in vegetable and water samples with high recovery rates.

Due to its outstanding ultrasensitivity, highly low detection limits and, optical sensing properties fluorescence has become a widespread choice for a variety of applications in different frontiers of science. The basic fluorescence quenching principle is often used, in which the addition of analyte causes decrease in emission intensity which is used as the sensing signal [73]. In some situations, the quenched fluorescence can be restored with competitive ligands, a process known as fluorescence switch "on and off" [74]. This phenomenon could be successfully used to quantify target analytes with high specificity [75]. To facilitate disruption of the first narrow range interaction between the fluorophore and the quencher, the analyte supplied must have a strong binding affinity for the quencher. The fluorophore will then be released from the quencher. As a result, fluorescence is restored, allowing for accurate detection of the target analyte. In single-step "turn-off" models, this additional step could reduce the chances of incorrect detection owing to environmental stimuli [76]. A good fluorophore that gives strong fluorescence signal, is not affected by photobleaching, and can be successfully quenched by a specified quencher, is an ideal choice for a fluorescence-based optical sensing system. In complicated matrices, this approach is used to analyse concentrations in the ng mL-1 range and, in some cases, the pg mL-1 range [77]. Despite all of the benefits of fluorescence technologies, only some kinds of chemicals exhibit native light as a result of deactivation events in the molecule. Furthermore, as compared to different analytical methods like UV/visible spectrophotometry, and chromatography the effects of absorption and scattering, limits the utilisation of fluorescence methods. Fluorescence, as one of the most common optical technologies, may be employed frequently in the fabrication of biosensors owning to its major properties such as high efficiency and sensitivity [78]. Table 4 summarizes the nanomaterials integrated fluorescence spectrophotometric techniques for the detection of different plant hormones.

Various phytohormones are detected using fluorescence mechanism. A novel report on the determination of ethylene concentration was done using fluorescence detection method. A paper based strategy employing Mn-doped SiO2 nanomaterial was used for the detection of ethylene [79]. The sensor depicts a broad linear range of 33–6667 ppm with detection limit of 10 ppm. The sensors show good results at room temperature with a good stability and had a future in the application of paper-based sensor in agronomy. For the detection of pyhtohormone GA, immunosensor was developed using the principle of fluorescence. In this method the AuNPs were conjugated with the antibody of GA and G-rich DNA ( Fig. 7a) [80]. The Fig. 7b shows the selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed method in presence of various interferences( another similar phytohormones). In the presence of GA, the GA-Ab-AuNPs gives a fluorescence signal. GA may be detected in concentrations ranging from 0.01 ng/mL to 70 ng/mL under ideal conditions, with a detection limit of 0.003 ng/mL. Furthermore, vegetable and fruit samples were tested using the developed immunoassay, with a recovery rate of 96.0–102.0%. The selectivity and repeatability of the sensor was assayed for further applications.

For plant inhibitory hormone ABA a novel strategy consisiting of CDs@ 2-methylimidazole zinc salt (CDs@ZIF8) and aptamer-fabricated AuNPs (Apt-AuNPs) was designed for the analysis of ABA by fluorescence method [81]. Due to the particular ABA-aptamer recognition, the addition of ABA caused the aggregation of AuNPs, which modified the fluorescence intensity of the probe (CDs@ZIF-8 @Apt-AuNPs). With a LOD of 30.0 ng/L, and a linear range of 0.100–150 ng/mL the developed probe is said to have a improved selectivity and sensitivity for detection of ABA contents in rice seeds. Another similar approach for detection of ABA uses CdSe/ZnS core shell NPs [82]. In this approach primary and secondary antibodies were functionalized on the NPs. The conjugate shows a good linear range of detection in the range of 1 pM to 10 nM. Another approach for the detection of synthetic auxin 2,4- D, employs the use of Pr3+ ion doped Gd2O3 NPs [83]. Fig. 8a depicts the fluorescence intensity of 5% Pr3+ ion doped-Gd2O3 NPs, it shows maximum emission intensity at 649 nm when excited at 283 nm and in presence of smilar herbicides, the 2,4 -D shows significant fluorescence quenching of 93%. Fig. 8b explains the sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method and Fig. 8c illustrates the fluorescence turn-off mechanism for the detection 2,4 -D. The electron transfer from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of 5% Pr3+ ion doped-Gd2O3 NPs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of 2,4- D is the possible reason behind the quenching. The conjugate shows a good LOD of 78 ppb and a linear range of 1–25 ppm. This analytical technique was also used in real water samples for the detection of the hormone. The high recovery percentage confirms the high effciency of the method. For the detection of same hormone (2,4- D) a fluorescence turn on method using quantum dots embedded silica NPs was employed [84]. In a reaction time of 5 min, the as-prepared fluorescence sensor achieved exceptional sensitivity with an impressive LOD of 0.14 µM, as well as distinct recognition selectivity for 2,4-D over its alternatives. The method was also applied in water samples showing a good recovery. A novel nanohybrid (N-doped CDs@Co-MOFs@molecular imprinting (MIP)) was fabricated and used as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of jasmonic acid in rice leaves [85]. Authors prepared dual emissive N-doped CDs, however they are inactive to jasmonic acid due to strong electrostatic repulsion. To change the surface charge of N-doped CDs, Co-MOFs was introduced into N-doped CDs, thereby offering high selectivity toward jasmonic acid. Then, N-doped CDs-Co-MOFs was used to pepare MIP probe for the generation of "key-lock" architecture to capture jasmonic acid with high specificity. It was noticed that the emission peak of N-doped CDs-Co-MOFs@MIP at 367 nm was decreased with increasing concentration of jasmonic acid, interestingly the emission intensity at 442 nm was progressively increased with increasing jasmonic acid. As a result, authors constructed two calibration graphs, showing good linearities in the concentration range of jasmonic acid (1–10 ng/mL and 10–800 ng/mL) with an impressive LOD of 0.35 ng/mL. The developed probe was applied to assay jasmonic acid in rice leaves, revealing that the N-doped CDs-Co-MOFs@MIP could be used for the estimation of phytohormones in crops.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of analytical techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Although the majority of conventional methods for detecting hormones in a variety of biological specimens, are sensitive, but necessitate expensive equipment and sophisticated research facilities. The major drawback of traditional analytical technique is taken over by using optical and electroanalytical methods. The electrochemical based detection have been widely used for the detection of plant hormones in food and environmental samples. Since electrochemical sensors exhibit several features such as miniaturization, portability, and easy application [86]. This method is also cost effective as it reduces the excessive expenses of labelling fluorescent probes. This technique can also be used for colloidal samples such as blood, food samples, soil sample etc. The major drawback of electroanalytical technique is its shelf life and disruptions in current signal in different environments. Electroanalytical technique based biosensors also faces variations in the result at different temperatures. To overcome these drawbacks, colorimetric sensors with good shelf life has been widely employed in the field of biosensors. In comparison to other visible detection techniques like immunoassay and enzyme-based detection, colorimetric sensors are fast, reliable and highly sensitive toward target plant hormones [87]. The visible color change reactions make this sensors user friendly and increases its feasibility. The reduced sample size, and minimal sample preparation makes it an ideal choice for monitoring of phytohormones in crops. These sensors have widely been used in the field of analytical and bioanalytical sciences due to real-time analysis and quick response time. The major disadvantage of this technique is that it can only be able to detect colored compounds. The colorless compounds cannot be analysed using this technique [88]. The colorimetric sensors cannot distinguish between target analyte and other interferring species present in the biological matrix. This eventually decreases the selectivity of the sensors. Furthermore, nanomaterials have been used as potential probes for fluorescence sensing of hormones. Due to the integration of nanomaterials, the use of fluorescent sensors has increased rapidly. The single-step reaction and biocompatibility of these sensors have received significant interest in assaying of various target molecules in agriculture sciences [89]. Fluorescent based sensors are highly sensitive and selective for the target analyte. They gives lowest detection limits which makes them a ideal candidate for detection in biofluids. However, there are some cons in fluorescence based technique, such as self- fluorescent materials present in the sample can hamper the sensitivity of the sensors. Additionally, it is also difficult to control the binding site of the target analyte, which can give false results [2,90]. Overall, every techniques have its own pros and cons, every technique has the potential to develop into a useful biosensor, but this promise must be realised through further clinical testing and optimisation. While numerous cutting-edge platforms of various kinds are now being developed, it is nevertheless crucial to take into account the characteristics required for a realistic implementation of functional nanomaterials as promising biosensors for phytohormones assays. The speed at which these nanosensors leave the lab and find their practical applications in the real world will be accelerated by an emphasis on attributes like specificity, timeliness, sensitivity, affordability and simplicity.

The hormones are present in trace amounts in the living bodies, which makes their detection challenging. With advancements in separation, sample preparation, and detection techniques, more accurate, sensitive, and high-throughput analytical approaches for tracing hormones are becoming available. With this comes the new challenges, including the discovery of new hormones and its specifications. These newly established analytical methods for hormone analysis offers their own set of benefits in terms of selectivity, sensitivity, and applicability. The major challenge for the researcher today is to develop the green methods for the extraction and purification of the hormones. This issue may be addressed in the near future, though several other different challenges in the development of other novel analytical methods requires prior attention. It is necessary to investigate upon the methods which detects multiple classes of hormones. For instance in the detection of phytohormones, all the classes of hormones are related to each other and its secretion and biosynthesis is based on mutual signalling of different hormones. Further, research must be focused on the development of eco-friendly approaches for the detection of multiple plant hormones, threby minimizing sample volume and preparations. Another major challenge in front of researchers is making the analytical- based detection methods automated. For high-throughout efficiency and good separation it is required to miniaturize the sensors, with an automated process. It would be beneficial in reducing the time, labour, human-based errors and extensive sample preparation. The development of integrated methods for pre-sample preparation and detection are still a major challenge for the researchers. In the field of hormone detection, mostly in living bodies, for the sample preparation, it is of ut-most important to develop non-invasive techniques. It is a key challenge in front of the bioanalyst to develop in-situ detection techniques as it faces various ethical problems. Nanomaterial based analytical techniques is an emerging field and have wide scope in conquering the above mentioned challenges in the near future. Hence forth it is predictable that development of new analytical techniques would play a key role in quantitative and qualitative detection of hormones.

In summary, we overviewed the classification of phytohormones and sample preparation tools for the extraction of phytohormones from various plants prior to their analysis by various analytical techniques. The recent progress on the functional nanostructure materials for the development of cost-effective portable analytical methods (electrochemical, colorimetry and fluorescence) for the detection of various plant hormones in plants nad environmental samples. The interesting outcomes are noticed by using various nanomaterials (CDs, NPs, MOFs, GO, QDs and NCs) in the development of electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for phytohormones assays that they have remarkable analytical features to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of colorimetry, fluorescence and electrochemical techniques without aid of tedious sample preparations. The detected phytohormone, type of functional nanomaterials, analytical characteristics (linear range and LOD) of phytohormone sensors (electrochemical, colorimetry and fluorescence) are summarized in Tables 2–4. The analytical advantages offered by nanomaterial integrated electrochemical, colorimetry and fluorescence techniques have successfully been monitored the levels of phytohormones in plants and environmental samples. Importantly, the integration of functional nanomaterials with the above analytical techniques for phytohormones assays not only improves sensitivity and selectivity but also minimize sample volumes and preparations. Overall, recent developments on the fabrication of functional nanomaterials and their integration with electrochemical and optical sensors have great prospectives in the development of eco-friendly nanobiosensors for monitoring of phytohormones in plants, which can help to enhance crop production in agriculture sectors.
The authors declare that there are no biological studies.
